content: The mean and variance of a binomial distribution are 4 and 2 respectively. Then the probability of exactly two successes is
explanation:
We have,

Mean, np = 4

and variance, npq = 2

$$\therefore$$ $${{npq} \over {np}} = {2 \over 4} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow q = {1 \over 2}$$ $$\therefore$$ $$ \Rightarrow p = {1 \over 2}$$ and n = 8

$$\therefore$$ $$P(x = 2) = {}^8{C_2}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^2}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^6}$$

$$={}^8{C_2}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^8}$$

$$ = {{8 \times 7} \over {2 \times 1}} \times {1 \over {256}} = {7 \over {64}}$$

content: Mean of n observations x<sub>1</sub>, x<sub>2</sub>, ...., x<sub>n</sub> is $$\overline x $$. If an observation x<sub>q</sub> is replaced by x<sub>q</sub>' then the new mean is
explanation:
We have,

$$\overline x = {{{x_1} + {x_2} + ... + {x_q} + ... + {x_n}} \over n}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sum x = n\overline x $$ ...(i)

If xq is replaced by $${{x_q}'}$$ then new total will be

$$\sum {x'} = \sum x - {x_q} - {x_q}'$$

$$ \therefore $$ New mean will be

$$\overline x ' = {{\sum {x'} } \over n}$$

$$\overline x ' = {{\sum x - {x_q} + {x_q}'} \over n}$$

$$\overline x ' = {{n\overline x - {x_q} + {x_q}'} \over n}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

content: Standard deviation of n observations a<sub>1</sub>, a<sub>2</sub>, a<sub>3</sub>, ....., a<sub>n</sub> is $$\sigma$$. Then, the standard deviation of the observations $$\lambda$$a<sub>1</sub>, $$\lambda$$a<sub>2</sub>, ....., $$\lambda$$a<sub>n</sub> is
explanation:
Let xi = a1, a2, a3, ...., an

and yi = $$\lambda$$a1, $$\lambda$$a2, $$\lambda$$a3, ......, $$\lambda$$an

$$ \therefore $$ yi = $$\lambda$$xi

$$ \because $$ $$\overline y = {{\sum {{y_i}} } \over N}$$

$$ = {{\sum {\lambda {x_i}} } \over n}$$ [$$ \because $$ $${y_i} = \lambda {x_i},N = n$$]

$$ = \lambda {{\sum {{x_i}} } \over n}$$

$$ = \lambda \overline x $$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\sigma _y} = \sqrt {{{\sum {{{({y_i} - \overline y )}^2}} } \over N}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{{\sum {{{(\lambda {x_i} - \lambda \overline x )}^2}} } \over n}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{{{\lambda ^2}\sum {{{({x_i} - \overline x )}^2}} } \over n}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{\lambda ^2}\sigma _x^2} = |\lambda |{\sigma _x} = |\lambda |\sigma $$
